users.bio: Media systems operator and guest on Episode 14.
Practical systems for small creator teams.
Metadata and RSS tooling for publishers.
Keyboard sound design and desk setup reviews.
Macro market commentary and weekly charts.
Indie game soundtrack clips and composer notes.
Home gardening and balcony herb experiments.

posts.text: Small podcast teams do not need more AI. They need fewer invisible handoffs. If your transcript, description, and clips all live in different places, publishing quality becomes luck.
@maya_chen Exactly. Most workflow debt is really state-management debt.
Metadata drift is one of the quietest ways to degrade a show. Your title, description, artwork, and chapters have to agree.
Rates, volatility, and positioning all moved before breakfast. Busy macro morning.
Swapped my desk monitors again. Still chasing the perfect nearfield setup for synth practice.
Layered strings plus tape hiss is still undefeated for moody menu music.
My basil recovered faster after I moved it away from the afternoon window.

🎙️ Episode 14 is live: Building a Reliable Podcast Production Pipeline

I sat down with @maya_chen to talk about how shows go from raw recording to polished episode—and why the pipeline matters.

🔗 https://creators.example.com/guests/maya-chen

Where teams overestimate automation:

• Editorial judgment (titles need audience awareness)
• Exception handling (guest corrections, legal concerns, sponsor constraints)

"Use automation for acceleration, not abdication."

Five stages every team needs:

1. Capture — recording + companion materials
2. Post-production — audio cleanup
3. Content extraction — transcripts, chapters, clips
4. Review — facts, names, links, sponsor compliance
5. Distribution — hosting, social, newsletter

Maya's rule: Treat transcripts as "rich input, not final truth."

Great for summaries, chapter suggestions, and quote candidates. But facts, names, affiliations, and links still need human review.

Maya's core insight: "The pipeline is the product."

Most listeners don't notice production—until something breaks. A vague title, sloppy transcript, or misaligned chapter markers all degrade trust.

Thanks to @soundlayer for sponsoring this episode—collaboration tools for small media teams organizing transcripts, assets, and review workflows.

🔗 https://creators.example.com/tools/soundlayer-overview

Practical fixes for small teams:

→ Standard folder structure + naming
→ One publish checklist, one owner
→ Separate raw and cleaned transcripts
→ Define your output bundle
→ Keep a lightweight episode log

🔗 https://creators.example.com/checklists/podcast-release

direct_messages.text: Happy to boost the episode once it's live.
